name: Hponkanrazi Wildlife Sanctuary

Fund Provider: Critical Ecosystem Partnership Fund (CEPF): Grant Amount 149973.00; Helmsley Charitable Trust: $50,000 (Upper Ayeyarwady Basin); GEF-Satoyama: $40,000 (Upper Ayeyarwady Basin); ACB-GIZ: $25,000 (Indawgyi)
Intermediaries [Roles]: FFI (lead implementer – fish surveys, community meetings, FCZ establishment, patrol/grant support); FoW (consultations, FCZ mapping/demarcation, awareness talks, landing surveys); NCSDAK (fish surveys); FISHBIO (led FCZ consultations, capacity building/training); FD/NWCD (authority, permitting, enforcement); DoF (FCZ notification/permits, amended State Freshwater Fisheries law for community roles).
Beneficiaries: Rawan and Lisu ethnic communities across 9 villages (Wasar Dam, Saw Law Di, Nam Ru, Awat Dam, Lone Na Dam, Wan Lai Dam, Khar Lan, Zi Yar Dam) plus wider Indawgyi community, Moe Nhyin District; 514 formally trained (356M/158F); 10 project/government/university/NGO staff trained in fish-survey and identification techniques; 95 fish species recorded in Indawgyi WS and 46 in Putao District/Mon Lan tributary, including threatened and endemic species (Microphis dunckeri, Schistura spp., Notopterus notopterus, Sperata acicularis, Wallago attu, Tor sp., Neolissochilus sp., Oreinus sp., Malihkaia spp.).
Focussed Area: Located in Myanmar's Upper Ayeyarwady Basin within Kachin State, this footprint spans the Indawgyi and Hponganrazi Wildlife Sanctuaries across the Moe Nhyin and Putao districts. Commodity [solution-oriented]: Fish Conservation Zones and Locally Managed Freshwater Areas; community fly-fishing ecotourism; KBA gap analysis; livelihood micro-grants; reviewed Fishing Restriction Zones; participatory patrolling and enforcement; amended State Freshwater Fisheries law.
Ecosystem Services: Provisioning: sustained fisheries, food security via spillover from FCZs, alternative livelihoods. Supporting: protected endemic fish populations and spawning/nursery habitats, water-quality maintenance, freshwater biodiversity. Cultural: fly-fishing ecotourism, indigenous knowledge integration.

Fund Provider: Critical Ecosystem Partnership Fund (CEPF) [$19,947]
Intermediaries [Roles]: Data not available in source file.
Beneficiaries: Data not available in source file.
Focussed Area: 10,000 hectares total (5,000 hectares at each site). Located at Naung Kwin Inn near Indawgyi Lake (Kachin State) and Naung Pho Mel village near Nam San (southern Shan State), with initial activities also in Maing Yaw village (northern Shan State).
Commodity [solution-oriented]: Vulture restaurants (diclofenac-free supplementary carcasses); bird-observation hides; local Vulture Protection Groups; school talks; village-authority engagement meetings; awareness signage; coverage in local weekly journals (The Voice, The 7 Days Journal).
Ecosystem Services: Supporting: vultures' ecological scavenger role, biodiversity conservation. Provisioning: N/A. Cultural: student ecosystem education, birdwatching/recreational value, shifting beliefs (dispelling bad-luck and bone-cure myths).
Classification_Rationale: Financed by non-governmental organizations, philanthropic foundations, or charities (identified via fund description).
Macro_Classification_Reason: Funds originate from non-governmental organizations, private foundations, or corporate entities.

name: Indawgyi Wildlife Sanctuary

Fund Provider: CEPF ($55,970.00); Oxfam ($5,850.00 - Project Coordinator: $2250, Mobilization Officer: $3600); TNGL ($23,750.00 - Office Place & Furniture: $5400, Office Equipment: $650, Equipment for field operation: $600, Stationary: $900, Project Coordinator: $9000, Mobilization Officer: $7200)
Intermediaries [Roles]: TNGL (implementer – patrolling model, CF activities, awareness campaigns, IEC materials); Forest Department (CF permits/certificates, technical guidance, biodiversity integration); Indawgyi WS (formal recognition, coaching, technical training); WCS (technical training, curriculum support); ALARM (IEC content support); FFI (awareness knowledge-sharing); village administration committees (community patrol support).
Beneficiaries: 4 Community Forest Users' Groups across 4 villages (Kardu, Mawhan, Zee Kone, Kankonegyi); 36 community leaders trained; 81 patrol members trained (Kardu 15, Mawhan 24, Zee Kone 30, Kankonegyi 12); 920 people (455M/465F) across 14 villages reached by awareness campaigns; Hoolock Gibbon protected.
Focussed Area: 3291 acres in total (Mawhan: 517 Acres, Kardu: 1417 Acres, Zee Kone: 619 Acres, Kankonegyi: 738 Acres), located in Indawgyi Watershed / Indawgyi Wildlife Sanctuary, Kachin State, Myanmar
Commodity [solution-oriented]: Community Forestry management plans and 30-year certificates; habitat management and monitoring protocols; patrolling rules; revolving funds; fire-prevention roads; IEC materials; community-based patrol groups.
Ecosystem Services: Supporting: biodiversity conservation, Hoolock gibbon habitat and food-tree protection, forest-fire control. Provisioning: N/A. Cultural: N/A.
Classification_Rationale: Financed by non-governmental organizations, philanthropic foundations, or charities (identified via fund description).
Macro_Classification_Reason: Funds originate from non-governmental organizations, private foundations, or corporate entities.

name: Kaylatha Wildlife Sanctuary
Fund Provider: Critical Ecosystem Partnership Fund (CEPF) (USD 20,000); Biodiversity and Nature Conservation Association (BANCA) (USD 3,941 in-kind for professional fees, travel, supplies, meeting, and support of equipment)
Intermediaries [Roles]: BANCA (institutional strengthening, technical support, capacity building, M&E, nursery, strategic/policy planning); Kelatha Forever Association (community outreach, implementation, local management); Forest Department (planting participation, permitting); Mon State Regional Government (permitting); IUCN (grant entity); Mr. Kyaw Sunn, plant taxonomist (Thawka-gyi survey); Maubin University (knowledge networking).
Beneficiaries: 33 formally trained (28M/5F); Win Ka village (33 people), Sin Inn (3), Kyar Si Aung (25); Taung Son and Kim villages; 3 coastal villages; 6 villages in Bilin Township; local villagers, students and teachers, ornithologists, Kelatha Forever staff, indigenous people.
Focussed Area: 2245.38 hectares in Kyeik Hto Township and Bilin Townships, Mon State, Myanmar (Center Latitude N17 ° 13' Longitude E97 ° 07')
Commodity [solution-oriented]: Organizational-development training (financial/office management); comprehensive survey of Thawka-gyi (Amherstia nobilis); biodiversity capacity building; birdwatching training; Financial, Administrative and HR policy development; 3-year Strategic Action Plan (2020–2022); planting of 11 tree species; awareness FGDs; nursery establishment and seedling production; community-based cultural/eco tourism; conservation incentive schemes; collaborative protected-area management.
Ecosystem Services: Supporting: habitat restoration in Kelatha Wildlife Sanctuary and coastal villages along the Gulf of Mottama, Thawka-gyi species conservation. Cultural: community-based cultural and eco-tourism, ecosystem-service education.
Classification_Rationale: Characterized by smaller-scale, short-term, or academically focused seed funding.
Macro_Classification_Reason: Funding is limited to small-scale seed capital or specialized academic research.

name: Meinmahla Kyun Wildlife Sanctuary
Fund Provider: KfW German Financial Cooperation (BMZ No. 2011 66 545) via ACB; EUR 581,027 total: mangrove restoration & livelihoods (EUR 366K), CMP (EUR 100K), law enforcement (EUR 90K) & conservation awareness (EUR 25K)
Intermediaries [Roles]: NWCD-MONREC (main implementing agency); Meinmahla Kyun Wildlife Sanctuary Park Management (on-site operations); grantees: Myanmar Environment Rehabilitation-conservation Network, Ecosystem Conservation and Community Development Initiative & Myint Myat Thu Doh Na Lone Tar; collaborators: local CBOs, ASEAN Centre for Biodiversity & GITEC Consulting Group
Beneficiaries: 441 households & 48 direct beneficiaries (alternative livelihoods & capacity building); local laborers (seedling raising & restoration ditching); estuarine crocodiles, freshwater turtles, shorebirds & critically endangered flora/fauna

Commodity [solution-oriented]: 193,770 seedlings across 18 native mangrove species; 12 village revolving fund committees for post-project business creation; 2,000 fuel-efficient stoves distributed; SMART co-patrolling, informant network & Village Ranger Programme; Mobile Awareness Conservation Unit; permanent sample plots on Meinmahla Island for plant regeneration monitoring
Ecosystem Services: Last Ayeyarwady Delta green space — storm risk reduction, critically endangered Sonneratia griffithii mangrove, Irrawaddy dolphin & long-tailed macaque (regulating & supporting); sustainable mangrove extraction & wetland education for delta communities (provisioning & cultural)
Classification_Rationale: Funded by a single country's government development agency (e.g., USAID, GIZ, BMUB).

name: Rakhine Yoma Elephant Range
Status Area: Wetland of International Importance (Ramsar Site); Wildlife Sanctuary; ASEAN Heritage Park
Fund Provider: CEPF (Critical Ecosystem Partnership Fund) - 103815.00 USD
Intermediaries [Roles]: WCS (grantee); Forest Department (KBA gap-analysis permitting, follow-up management); GAD (follow-up KBA management); 18 local CSOs/networks and Zoology Dept Sittwe University (key-informant interviews, FGDs, multistakeholder workshops); villagers (same participatory roles).
Beneficiaries: Villagers, indigenous peoples, local communities and CSOs across Chin Hills Complex, Rakhine Yoma Range and Western Shan Yoma Range (household counts not specified); globally threatened (CR/EN/VU) mammals and birds across 36 Key Biodiversity Areas.
Focussed Area: Spanning three major conservation corridors in Myanmar—the Chin Hills Complex (13 KBAs), Rakhine Yoma Range (16 KBAs), and Western Shan Yoma Range (7 KBAs)—this network encompasses 36 distinct KBAs. These protected sites vary significantly in scale, ranging from the compact La Mu Area (4,646 hectares) to expansive reserves like the Paung Laung Watershed Area (255,390 hectares), Rakhine Yoma Elephant Range (170,640 hectares), and Natmataung National Park (109,220 hectares).
Commodity [solution-oriented]: Key informant interviews, focus group discussions and multistakeholder workshops for KBA gap analysis; ICCA collaborative mechanism; corridor communication/participation plans; model KBA management for replication; proposed policy revisions submitted to government.
Ecosystem Services: Freshwater security and essential NTFP harvesting for indigenous communities (provisioning); flood mitigation, carbon storage, and critical erosion control across the fragile Chin Hills (regulating); continuous habitat connectivity across 36 KBAs for globally threatened taxa (supporting); and the preservation of ethnic heritage through the establishment of community-conserved territories (cultural).
Classification_Rationale: Financed by non-governmental organizations, philanthropic foundations, or charities (identified via fund description).
Macro_Classification_Reason: Funds originate from non-governmental organizations, private foundations, or corporate entities.

Classification_Rationale: Combines multiple sources of funding, such as international grants, local government allocations, or private investments.
Macro_Classification_Reason: Combines capital from multiple sectors (public, private, NGO) or uses market-based collaborative mechanisms.

name: Tanintharyi Nature Reserve
Fund Provider: Moattama Gas Transportation Company (MGTC/Total), Taninthayi Pipeline Company (TPC/Petronas) & Andaman Transportation Limited (ATL/PTTEP) — voluntary commercial compensation, $600,000/company per 4-year phase: Phase 1 (2005–2009) $1.2M (MGTC & TPC); Phase 2 (2009–2013) $1.2M (MGTC & TPC); Phase 3 (2013–2016) $1.8M (MGTC, TPC & ATL)

Phase 2)
